content.text: Software as a Service (SaaS) is a cloud delivery model in which the provider operates the complete application stack — from physical servers through the OS, runtime, application code, and data storage — and delivers it as a ready-to-use product over the internet. You sign in, configure settings, and use the product. You do not deploy code, manage servers, or maintain a database.

SaaS is the endpoint of the cloud service model spectrum. IaaS gives you virtualized infrastructure to manage; PaaS gives you a platform to deploy your code onto; SaaS gives you a finished product to use. The only things you own are
your data and who can access it

Common SaaS examples outside AWS: Gmail, Salesforce, Slack, GitHub. AWS-native SaaS products include Amazon WorkMail, Amazon QuickSight, Amazon Connect, and Amazon Chime. Additionally, thousands of third-party SaaS applications are sold through AWS Marketplace, licensed per-seat and billed through your AWS account.

SaaS eliminates the total cost of ownership for running software that is not a core differentiator. An engineering team does not need to host its own email server, build a business intelligence platform, or operate a contact center — all of these are solved problems that SaaS vendors have already scaled globally.

For builders, SaaS matters in two directions. First, you consume SaaS products (GitHub, Datadog, PagerDuty, Snowflake) as part of your engineering toolchain — this is standard practice. Second, if you build software for others, you are likely building a SaaS product yourself, and understanding the model informs decisions about multi-tenancy, data isolation, subscription pricing, and operations.
No servers to patch, no database to tune, no capacity to plan. The SaaS provider handles all infrastructure 24/7.
SaaS products are typically hosted in multiple regions with built-in CDN delivery. A new user in any geography gets a fast, available service on day one.
SaaS pricing is usually subscription-based (monthly per seat or per unit of use), making budgeting straightforward and eliminating upfront capital costs.
The provider rolls out features and security patches without requiring action from you. You always use the current version.
SaaS products run in a browser or thin client, making them accessible from any device without local installation.

Mature SaaS products expose APIs and webhooks. AWS services like EventBridge SaaS integrations, AppFlow, and API Gateway can connect your AWS workloads to SaaS data sources without building custom integrations from scratch.

You cannot choose the underlying instance type, tune the database query planner, or control deployment timing. The provider decides when to roll out a new version, and your only option is to accept it.

Your data resides in the provider's infrastructure. Highly regulated industries (healthcare, financial services) must verify that the SaaS product's data residency, encryption practices, and compliance certifications match their requirements.

Migrating away from a SaaS product means exporting your data, re-training users, and replacing integrations — all at once. Data portability (bulk export, standard formats) should be evaluated before committing to a vendor.

SaaS products serve many tenants with a common codebase. Deep customizations beyond configuration, themes, or extension points are not possible. If your workflow diverges significantly from what the product supports, you may end up fighting the tool.
Per-seat or per-API-call pricing can become expensive at scale. At a certain user count it may be cheaper to build and host the equivalent feature internally on IaaS.
How SaaS Works — Architecture and Shared Responsibility

The diagram below shows the shared responsibility model for SaaS. Compared to IaaS or PaaS, the customer-managed zone shrinks to just data and access control. The provider manages everything else — application logic, runtime, OS, network, and physical infrastructure.

attrs.alt: SaaS shared responsibility diagram — customer owns only data and access; provider manages everything else
attrs.title: SaaS Shared Responsibility Model

content.text: A SaaS product typically runs as a
: many customers share the same application servers and database cluster, with data isolation enforced at the application or row-level by the provider. Some enterprise SaaS products offer
options (often called "private cloud" or "VPC peering" plans) for customers with strict data isolation requirements, but these cost significantly more.

QuickSight is AWS's managed business intelligence (BI) service. You connect it to data sources (S3, RDS, Redshift, Athena, Salesforce), build dashboards, and share them with stakeholders — all without running a BI server. Pricing is per-reader per-month (SPICE-based analytics) or per-session. The underlying EC2 fleet, Redshift-like query engine, and CDN delivery are fully opaque to users.

Connect is a cloud contact center SaaS. Agents log in to a browser-based softphone and handle calls routed by Connect's IVR and automatic call distribution engine. You pay per-minute of telephony usage and per-active-agent, with no contact center infrastructure to manage. Connect integrates with Lambda for custom IVR logic, DynamoDB for customer data lookups, and Lex for conversational AI.

WorkMail is a managed email and calendar service compatible with Microsoft Outlook and standard IMAP clients. AWS manages the Exchange-compatible backend, storage, and spam filtering. You provision mailboxes, set quota limits, and configure mail domains — nothing more.

AWS Marketplace hosts hundreds of third-party SaaS products — Datadog, Splunk, Snowflake, MongoDB Atlas, New Relic, Trend Micro — that bill through your AWS account. This simplifies procurement (single invoice, AWS credits eligible), though the products themselves run on the vendor's own infrastructure, not yours.
Integrating SaaS with Your AWS Workloads
Even though SaaS runs outside your AWS account, AWS provides multiple integration patterns to connect SaaS data and events to your workloads.

AppFlow is a no-code integration service that moves data between SaaS applications (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Slack, Google Analytics, SAP) and AWS services (S3, Redshift, EventBridge). You configure a flow in the Console and AppFlow handles authentication, pagination, and data transformation.

EventBridge supports SaaS partner event sources. Vendors like Zendesk, PagerDuty, Auth0, and Stripe can publish events directly to an EventBridge event bus in your account. You then write rules that route those events to Lambda, SQS, Step Functions, or other targets — building event-driven workflows without polling SaaS APIs.

# List available SaaS partner event sources
aws events list-partner-event-source-names --name-prefix "" --region us-east-1

# Create an EventBridge rule that triggers Lambda when a Stripe payment.failed event arrives
aws events put-rule \
  --name stripe-payment-failed \
  --event-pattern '{
    "source": ["aws.partner/stripe.com"],
    "detail-type": ["payment.failed"]
  }' \
  --state ENABLED

aws events put-targets \
  --rule stripe-payment-failed \
  --targets '[{
    "Id": "HandlePaymentFailed",
    "Arn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:handle-payment-failed"
  }]'
API Gateway as SaaS Webhook Receiver

Many SaaS products push events as webhooks (HTTP POST to a URL you provide). A common AWS pattern is to receive webhooks at API Gateway, validate the payload signature (e.g., Stripe's HMAC-SHA256), and enqueue the event to SQS for durable, at-least-once processing by Lambda.

import hashlib
import hmac
import json
import os
import boto3

sqs = boto3.client('sqs')
QUEUE_URL = os.environ['WEBHOOK_QUEUE_URL']
SECRET = os.environ['STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET'].encode()

def handler(event, context):
    # Validate Stripe HMAC-SHA256 signature before trusting the payload
    sig_header = event['headers'].get('stripe-signature', '')
    payload = event['body'].encode()

    # Stripe signature header: t=<timestamp>,v1=<hex_digest>
    parts = dict(item.split('=', 1) for item in sig_header.split(',') if '=' in item)
    timestamp = parts.get('t', '')
    expected = parts.get('v1', '')

    signed_payload = f"{timestamp}.{event['body']}".encode()
    computed = hmac.new(SECRET, signed_payload, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()

    if not hmac.compare_digest(computed, expected):
        return {'statusCode': 400, 'body': 'Invalid signature'}

    sqs.send_message(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        MessageBody=event['body'],
        MessageAttributes={
            'EventType': {
                'StringValue': json.loads(event['body']).get('type', 'unknown'),
                'DataType': 'String'
            }
        }
    )
    return {'statusCode': 200, 'body': 'ok'}
If you are building a multi-tenant SaaS product on AWS, several architectural concerns are unique to the SaaS model.
Each tenant gets a dedicated AWS account or VPC with their own resources. Maximum isolation and compliance, but highest cost and operational overhead. Used for high-security enterprise deals.
All tenants share the same RDS database. A

column in every table scopes data to the tenant. Application code enforces the filter on every query. Cheapest and most scalable, but requires rigorous application-layer guard-rails to prevent data leakage.
Application servers are shared, but each tenant gets their own DynamoDB table or S3 prefix/bucket. Balances cost and isolation.

For DynamoDB-backed SaaS, you can use IAM condition keys to enforce tenant isolation at the IAM level rather than at the application layer, preventing bugs from leaking tenant data even if application code is wrong.
When you assume a role scoped to the tenant's ID (via STS with a session tag for

), the IAM condition restricts DynamoDB access to only the partition key values that match that tenant. A bug in application code cannot accidentally read another tenant's rows — the IAM policy rejects the call at the AWS API layer.
A 50-person software company adopts a pure SaaS toolchain for its engineering and business operations:
for source control and CI/CD — no self-hosted GitLab to maintain.
for observability — metrics, traces, and logs consolidated without running an ELK stack.
for on-call scheduling — integrates with Datadog alerts via EventBridge SaaS event source.
for CRM — AppFlow syncs contacts nightly to Redshift for revenue analysis in QuickSight.
for payments — webhooks land at API Gateway → Lambda → SQS pipeline. Stripe's HMAC signature is validated before any event is trusted.

This company runs 0 email servers, 0 monitoring agents to manage, 0 CI machines to patch. The engineering team focuses entirely on the product. The trade-off is monthly SaaS bills totaling ~$8,000/month — well below the engineering cost of running equivalent self-hosted infrastructure.
Not reviewing data residency before signing a SaaS contract.

Many SaaS vendors default to US-based data storage. Regulated industries in the EU, Australia, or Canada may face GDPR, APPs, or PIPEDA violations if customer data is stored in the wrong jurisdiction. Check data residency options — including subprocessors — in the vendor's Data Processing Agreement before onboarding.

A SaaS vendor has access to your data. At minimum, obtain and review the vendor's SOC 2 Type II report before sending sensitive data. Also review their subprocessor list — your data may pass through multiple third parties.

Webhooks arrive from the internet. Without signature verification, an attacker can POST fake events to your endpoint to manipulate your system (e.g., fabricating a payment.succeeded event). Always validate HMAC or JWT signatures using the secret shared during webhook setup.
Row-level security enforced only in application code.

Application bugs (missing WHERE clause, ORM misconfiguration) can expose cross-tenant data. Use IAM condition keys, Row Security Policies in PostgreSQL (RDS), or DynamoDB condition expressions to add a defense-in-depth layer below the application.

Vendor contracts end. If you have not confirmed how to bulk-export your data (format, API limits, cost), you may face a difficult migration. Verify export capabilities before signing, and schedule a periodic export test.
Evaluate SaaS products on their security posture.
Look for SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and CSA STAR certifications. Enterprise SaaS products publish security documentation; request it before procurement.

Connect SaaS products to your corporate identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, or IAM Identity Center) via SAML 2.0 or OIDC. Enable SCIM to automate user provisioning and de-provisioning — when an employee leaves, their SaaS access is revoked automatically.
Use AppFlow or EventBridge for SaaS integrations.

Avoid building custom polling integrations. AppFlow handles pagination, incremental sync, and OAuth token refresh. EventBridge SaaS event sources deliver events in real time without custom webhook infrastructure.
For SaaS you build: implement tenant isolation at multiple layers.
Application code filters by tenant_id, database row security policies enforce it, and IAM conditions back-stop it. Defense in depth prevents a single bug from causing a cross-tenant data breach.
Prefer metered over flat-rate pricing when building SaaS.
Metered pricing (per-API-call, per-GB, per-active-user) aligns your cost with your customers' usage and AWS Marketplace metering billing is a ready-made way to implement this.

SaaS reduces but does not eliminate your security surface. The AWS Shared Responsibility Model applies to every layer the provider manages, but several responsibilities remain yours regardless of the model.

In SaaS you control who can access the product and what they can do. Failing to enforce least privilege at the SaaS layer — giving everyone admin access, not revoking access when an employee leaves, using a shared service account — creates the same risk as over-broad IAM policies in your AWS account.
Any SaaS product handling business data should require MFA. Use SSO via IAM Identity Center, Okta, or Azure AD so MFA is enforced at the identity provider level rather than per-product.

When an employee's account is deactivated in your identity provider, SCIM propagates the deactivation to all connected SaaS products within minutes. Without SCIM, off-boarding requires manual steps in each product — often missed.

Enterprise SaaS products export audit logs (login events, data exports, permission changes) to S3 or a SIEM. Ship these to CloudWatch Logs or your SIEM and alert on anomalies — large data exports outside business hours, logins from unexpected geolocations.
Not all data is appropriate for a given SaaS product. Before enabling a SaaS integration, classify the data it will access:
— appropriate for any SaaS product with reasonable security posture.
(customer PII, financial records) — requires SOC 2 Type II, BAA (if HIPAA), DPA (if GDPR), and confirmed data residency in the correct jurisdiction.
(PHI, card data, classified) — in many cases SaaS is not appropriate at all; self-hosted or single-tenant dedicated environments are required.

Each SaaS product you adopt is a third-party that has access to some portion of your data or systems. Maintain a SaaS inventory, review security certifications annually, and confirm the vendor's incident response process — specifically, do they notify affected customers within 72 hours as required by GDPR Article 33?
SaaS performance is determined by the provider, not by you. This is both a benefit and a constraint.

SaaS products publish SLAs (typically 99.9% or 99.95% monthly uptime). Read these carefully — they define what constitutes downtime, how credits are calculated, and how to claim them. Many SLAs exclude scheduled maintenance and define "downtime" narrowly (e.g., complete service unavailability, not degraded performance).

SaaS APIs enforce rate limits — typically requests-per-second or requests-per-day per token. If your integration hits rate limits, requests fail or queue. Build exponential backoff and retry logic in any SaaS API client.

Subscribe to the vendor's status page (or use a service like Betteruptime) to receive incident notifications. Map SaaS status to your own observability dashboards — an outage in a critical SaaS dependency should surface in your on-call alerting.

For SaaS integrations that are in the critical path of your application (e.g., an auth SaaS, a payment SaaS), implement circuit breakers. If the SaaS endpoint returns errors above a threshold, stop sending requests and return a cached response or a graceful degradation message until the circuit resets.

SaaS sprawl — accumulating dozens of subscriptions without oversight — creates cost and security problems. Shadow IT (employees signing up for SaaS with personal credit cards) means data is outside the company's control and compliance review.
Route all SaaS purchases through AWS Marketplace where available — consolidated billing, AWS credits may apply, and vendor security reviews are pre-completed for Marketplace listings.
Track SaaS spend alongside AWS spend.
AWS Marketplace SaaS charges appear in your AWS Cost Explorer alongside EC2 and S3 costs. Tag Marketplace subscriptions with cost-center tags to allocate SaaS costs to the correct team.
Per-seat SaaS billing scales linearly with headcount. Audit active users quarterly and remove inactive seats. Many SaaS products bill for licensed seats regardless of usage.

At sufficient scale or with sufficiently unique requirements, building in-house on IaaS/PaaS may be cheaper or more capable than the SaaS alternative. This calculation changes as team size and engineering cost evolve.
IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS — Full Comparison
Continuing the transportation analogy used for IaaS (renting a car) and PaaS (hiring a driver):

You buy a ticket, board, and arrive at your destination. You do not know the engine type, fuel source, driver schedule, or maintenance plan. Your only decisions are which train to board (vendor selection) and where to get off (configuration). The train runs whether or not you are on it, and every passenger is on the same train.

The value proposition is clear: you do not need to know how a train works to benefit from it. The cost is equally clear: you cannot re-route it, customize the seats, or take a detour the train does not make.
SaaS is the highest-abstraction cloud model: you consume a finished product and manage only your data and user access.
AWS SaaS products include QuickSight (BI), Connect (contact center), WorkMail (email), and hundreds of Marketplace third-party ISV products.
AppFlow and EventBridge SaaS event sources are the primary AWS-native integration paths for pulling data from or reacting to events in external SaaS products.
Always validate webhook signatures (HMAC) before trusting SaaS event payloads — use API Gateway + Lambda for durable webhook receipt.
When building SaaS yourself, implement tenant isolation at multiple layers: application code, database row security, and IAM condition keys.
Key SaaS risks: data sovereignty, vendor lock-in, and cross-tenant data leakage. Address them through due diligence (SOC 2, DPA), SSO+SCIM, and defense-in-depth isolation.
